title: ACC 210 — Concepts of Financial Reporting
description: The full ACC 210 curriculum at NC State. Master the accounting cycle, financial statements, and the core concepts every accountant needs — from the accounting equation through financial ratio analysis.
subject: Accounting

learn.explanation: Financial statements are the scoreboard of a business. Every time a company buys inventory, pays an employee, or collects from a customer, those events eventually show up in one of four reports: the income statement (did we make money?), the balance sheet (what do we own and owe right now?), the statement of owner's equity (how has the owner's stake changed?), and the statement of cash flows (where did the cash actually go?). Bankers look at these before approving a loan. Investors study them before buying stock. If you own or manage a business, these four reports are the most honest summary of how things are actually going.
learn.example: Apple publishes four financial statements each quarter. Their income statement shows revenues of $94.9 billion and net income of $21.7 billion for a single quarter. The balance sheet, dated the last day of that quarter, shows total assets of $352 billion. These numbers let investors compare Apple's financial health to prior periods and to competitors like Microsoft.
keyPoints: Four statements, four questions: income statement (profitable?), balance sheet (what do we own and owe?), equity statement (owner's stake change?), cash flow statement (where did cash go?)
The income statement and cash flow statement cover a period of time; the balance sheet is a snapshot on one specific date
These statements are used by lenders, investors, and managers to make real decisions — accurate reporting directly affects your cost of capital and your business reputation

learn.explanation: The legal structure you choose for your business determines how much personal risk you carry if things go wrong. A sole proprietorship is just you — easy to set up, but your personal bank account and house are on the line if the business gets sued. A partnership works the same way but with multiple owners. A corporation is legally its own separate person: if it fails or gets sued, shareholders lose only what they invested, not their personal assets. That liability protection is why most serious businesses eventually incorporate, even though it comes with more paperwork and higher taxes.
learn.example: Maria runs a freelance graphic design business by herself — that is a sole proprietorship. She and a friend decide to open a bakery together, splitting profits 50/50 — that is a partnership. When they want to expand and take on outside investors without risking their personal savings, they incorporate, forming Maria & Jane Bakery Inc. — a corporation where shareholders are only liable up to the amount they invested.
keyPoints: Corporations provide limited liability — the single biggest reason most growing businesses eventually incorporate
Sole proprietorships are easiest to start but put your personal assets at risk if the business is sued or fails
Tax treatment, liability exposure, and ability to raise outside capital all differ significantly across the three forms — structure matters from day one

learn.explanation: The accounting equation — Assets = Liabilities + Owner's Equity — is the one rule that can never be violated. Assets are everything the business controls that has value: cash, inventory, equipment, land. Liabilities are what the business owes to outsiders: bank loans, unpaid invoices, credit cards. Owner's equity is what's left over for the owner after all debts are paid. Think of it this way: if you liquidated every asset and paid off every debt, whatever cash remained would be the owner's equity. Every single transaction in accounting, no matter how complex, is just moving numbers around this equation while keeping both sides equal.
learn.example:
A new company has $50,000 in cash funded by a $30,000 bank loan and $20,000 invested by the owner.
Accounting equation check:
Assets $50,000 = Liabilities $30,000 + Owner's Equity $20,000 ✓
Both sides balance at $50,000.
keyPoints: Assets = Liabilities + Owner's Equity — this equation never breaks, under any circumstance
Equity is what the owner would have left after paying off every debt — it is the true measure of the owner's stake
Every transaction moves numbers within this equation while keeping both sides equal — if yours don't balance, something was recorded wrong

topics.title: Transaction Analysis and the Accounting Equation

learn.explanation: Every transaction that touches your business affects at least two accounts simultaneously — that is the whole premise of double-entry bookkeeping. When you borrow money from a bank, your cash goes up AND your debt goes up. When a customer pays you, cash goes up AND the revenue (or receivable) goes down. Before recording any transaction, ask yourself two questions: which accounts are affected, and do they go up or down? The equation must stay balanced after every single entry. If you ever find it out of balance, you have an error somewhere.
learn.example:
A company borrows $10,000 from a bank.
Analysis: Cash increases $10,000 (asset up), Notes Payable increases $10,000 (liability up).
Before: Assets $50,000 = Liabilities $30,000 + Equity $20,000
After:  Assets $60,000 = Liabilities $40,000 + Equity $20,000 ✓
keyPoints: Every transaction affects at least two accounts — that is why it is called double-entry bookkeeping
Before recording anything, identify which accounts change and whether they increase or decrease
If the accounting equation is not balanced after a transaction, you made an error — there is no such thing as a one-sided entry

learn.explanation: Financial statements exist because people outside the business need reliable information to make decisions. A bank deciding whether to give you a $2 million loan will spend hours on your financial statements — particularly your balance sheet (do you have assets backing the loan?) and your cash flow statement (can you actually service the debt?). An investor deciding whether to buy your stock focuses on the income statement (are you growing profitably?) and compares your numbers to competitors. Employees, suppliers, regulators, and tax authorities all have their own reasons to care. How you report your numbers affects every relationship your business has with the outside world.
learn.example: When Amazon applies for a $500 million credit line from a bank, the bank's loan officers study Amazon's balance sheet (to check existing debt levels) and cash flow statement (to verify Amazon generates enough cash to repay). Meanwhile, a retail investor watching Amazon stock studies the income statement to see whether net income is growing year over year.
keyPoints: Creditors care most about your ability to repay — they focus on the balance sheet and the cash flow statement
Investors care about profitability and growth — they study income statement trends and compare you to industry peers
Financial statements for public companies are public information — how you report affects your borrowing costs, your stock price, and your business reputation

learn.explanation: The chart of accounts is the master list of every bucket your business uses to track money. Think of it as the filing system behind your financial records. Every time something happens financially — you pay rent, sell a product, take out a loan — it goes into one or more specific accounts from this list. Accounts are grouped and numbered by type: assets start with 1, liabilities with 2, equity with 3, revenues with 4, and expenses with 5. A well-designed chart of accounts makes it easy to answer questions fast: 'how much did we spend on marketing last quarter?' 'how much do customers currently owe us?' The chart you build at the start shapes how useful your financial reports will be.
learn.example:
A small restaurant's chart of accounts:
101 Cash
110 Accounts Receivable
141 Food Inventory
201 Accounts Payable
301 Owner's Capital
401 Food Revenue
501 Food Cost Expense
510 Rent Expense
520 Wages Expense
When the restaurant pays rent, it records: Debit 510 Rent Expense / Credit 101 Cash.
keyPoints: Accounts are numbered by type: 100s = assets, 200s = liabilities, 300s = equity, 400s = revenue, 500s = expenses
Every financial transaction gets recorded in accounts from the chart — it is your entire financial filing system
A well-organized chart of accounts lets you pull any financial data instantly — the structure you build at the start determines the quality of your reporting forever

learn.explanation: Debits and credits are the left and right sides of every account — they have no inherent positive or negative meaning on their own. The effect depends on which type of account you are working with. Debits increase assets and expenses; credits increase liabilities, equity, and revenues. Credits decrease assets and expenses; debits decrease liabilities, equity, and revenues. The most important rule: every journal entry must have total debits equal to total credits, always. If they don't balance, you have an error. Most students find this confusing at first, but after recording a few dozen transactions it becomes automatic.
learn.example:
A company pays $500 cash for office supplies.
Office Supplies (asset) increases → Debit Office Supplies $500
Cash (asset) decreases → Credit Cash $500

Journal entry:
Debit  Office Supplies  500
Credit Cash             500
Total debits $500 = Total credits $500 ✓
keyPoints: Debits increase assets and expenses; credits increase liabilities, equity, and revenues — memorize this before anything else
Every journal entry must have equal total debits and total credits — if they don't balance, you have a recording error
Debit and credit just mean left and right — they carry no inherent positive or negative meaning

topics.title: Journal Entries and the General Journal

learn.explanation: A journal entry is the official first record of any business transaction. Before a transaction touches any financial report, it starts here — written in chronological order in the general journal. Every entry has a date, the accounts debited and credited, the dollar amounts, and a short description explaining what happened. The debited account is listed first, flush to the left. The credited account is indented below it. The description is not optional — if an auditor or your future self reads this entry, they should immediately understand what the transaction was and why it happened.
learn.example:
June 1: A company receives $1,000 cash from a customer for services just rendered.

Date        Account               Debit   Credit
June 1      Cash                  1,000
              Service Revenue               1,000
  (Received cash for services performed)

Debit Cash because cash (asset) increased.
Credit Service Revenue because revenue increased.
keyPoints: Journal entries are the first official record of every transaction — accuracy here flows through every subsequent report
Debited account is listed first, flush left; credited account is indented below — this format is universal
Always include a description clear enough that someone reading it months later knows exactly what happened

learn.explanation: After journal entries are recorded, every debit and credit gets transferred to the relevant T-account in the general ledger — a process called posting. A T-account is just a visual tool shaped like the letter T: the account name at the top, all debits on the left side, all credits on the right. The ledger holds one T-account for every account in your chart of accounts, giving you a running total for each. If you want to know the current balance of any account — your bank account, what you owe suppliers, total sales for the month — you look in the ledger. It is the source of truth for every number that ends up on your financial statements.
learn.example:
The Cash T-account:

          CASH
  Debit  |  Credit
  10,000 |
   2,000 |    500
---------+--------
Left side total: $12,000
Right side total: $500
Balance: $12,000 - $500 = $11,500 (debit balance)
keyPoints: T-accounts show every debit on the left and every credit on the right — the balance is the difference between the two sides
The general ledger is the master record of every account balance — every financial statement number traces back to it
Posting errors — transferring the wrong amount or to the wrong account — will throw off your trial balance and must be tracked down

learn.explanation: A trial balance is a quick sanity check: list every account and its balance, add up the debit column, add up the credit column, and confirm they match. If they don't match, you have a math or posting error somewhere that must be fixed before moving on. If they do match, that is a necessary condition — but not a guarantee — that your records are correct. You can still have errors that don't break the balance, like debiting the wrong expense account or forgetting an entire transaction. The trial balance is run at least twice: once before adjusting entries (unadjusted) and once after (adjusted), where the adjusted version becomes the source for the financial statements.
learn.example:
Month-end trial balance:
Account                  Debit    Credit
Cash                     5,000
Supplies                   200
Equipment               10,000
Accounts Payable                  2,000
Owner's Capital                  13,200
Totals                  15,200   15,200 ✓
keyPoints: A balanced trial balance confirms debits equal credits — but it does not mean your books are error-free
Normal balances: assets and expenses carry debit balances; liabilities, equity, and revenues carry credit balances
An out-of-balance trial balance means you have a math or posting error that must be found before preparing any financial statements

learn.explanation: The accounting cycle is the repeating sequence of steps every business follows to go from raw transactions to finished financial statements. It has eight steps: analyze transactions, record journal entries, post to the ledger, prepare a trial balance, record adjusting entries, prepare the adjusted trial balance, prepare financial statements, and record closing entries. Then the whole thing starts over for the next period. Understanding this cycle matters because every financial report a business produces comes from following these steps correctly. Skip a step or do it out of order and your statements will be wrong — sometimes by a little, sometimes by a lot.
learn.example:
At ABC Company each month:
1. Collect receipts and invoices (analyze transactions)
2. Record them as journal entries
3. Post debits and credits to T-accounts in the ledger
4. List all balances to check they are equal (trial balance)
5. Adjust for depreciation, accruals, and deferrals
6. Prepare an updated trial balance
7. Produce the income statement and balance sheet
8. Zero out revenue and expense accounts (closing entries)
keyPoints: Eight steps, every period — mastering the sequence means you always know exactly where you are in the process
Steps 1 through 4 happen throughout the period; steps 5 through 8 happen at period-end
Every number on your financial statements flows from this cycle — skip a step and the statements will be wrong

learn.explanation: Cash basis accounting is simple: record income when cash comes in, record expenses when cash goes out. The problem is that it gives you a distorted picture of performance. A business can have a great January on paper under cash basis simply because a big invoice from November finally got paid. Accrual accounting fixes this by recording revenue when it is earned — when you do the work or deliver the goods — and recording expenses when they are incurred, regardless of when cash moves. GAAP requires accrual for most businesses, and any bank or investor will expect accrual-based financials. Learning to think in accrual terms is one of the most important mental shifts in accounting.
learn.example:
A consulting firm completes a $5,000 project in December and bills the client, who pays in January.

Accrual basis: Record $5,000 revenue in December (when earned).
Cash basis: Record $5,000 revenue in January (when cash received).

The accrual method gives a more accurate picture of December's performance and is required under GAAP.
keyPoints: Accrual records revenue when earned and expenses when incurred — not when cash moves
Cash basis is simpler but gives a distorted picture of performance, especially for businesses with payment timing gaps
GAAP requires accrual accounting — any bank loan application or investor due diligence will require accrual-basis financials

learn.explanation: The revenue recognition principle answers a deceptively simple question: when have you actually earned the money? The answer is not when you invoice, not when cash arrives, and not when a contract is signed. Revenue is earned when you have delivered the promised goods or performed the promised service. This matters enormously in practice. A gym that sells annual memberships in January has not earned twelve months of revenue on January 1 — it earns a portion each month as it keeps the gym open and accessible. Recording all of it upfront would overstate income and mislead anyone reading the financials.
learn.example:
A gym collects $600 from a member in January for a one-year membership. The gym has not yet earned all $600 — it earns $50 per month as service is provided.

January entry: Debit Cash $600, Credit Unearned Revenue $600
Each month's adjusting entry: Debit Unearned Revenue $50, Credit Membership Revenue $50

After 12 months, all $600 is earned and Unearned Revenue is $0.
keyPoints: Record revenue when you have delivered the goods or performed the service — not when you invoice and not when you get paid
Cash received before service is performed is a liability called Unearned Revenue — you owe the customer something
Revenue manipulation is one of the most common forms of accounting fraud — this principle exists specifically to prevent it

learn.explanation: The matching principle says that expenses should appear on the income statement in the same period as the revenues they helped generate. If you spent money to produce this period's revenue, that cost belongs this period — even if the cash goes out next month. This is what makes the income statement tell the truth about profitability. Without it, you could make one period look fantastic simply by delaying payments, even if the underlying business activity was the same. Depreciation, accrued wages, prepaid insurance amortization — these all exist because of the matching principle.
learn.example:
A company pays $12,000 for a one-year insurance policy on January 1.

Without the matching principle: record $12,000 expense in January.
With the matching principle: record $1,000 expense each month.

Adjusting entry each month:
Debit Insurance Expense  1,000
Credit Prepaid Insurance  1,000

This spreads the cost across the 12 months that benefit from the coverage.
keyPoints: Match expenses to the revenues they helped generate — not to the period when cash was paid
If you pay for something that benefits multiple periods, allocate the cost across those periods proportionally
This principle is why depreciation, prepaid expense adjustments, and accrued liabilities all exist in accounting

learn.explanation: At the end of every accounting period, before you prepare financial statements, you need to clean up the books. Some revenues have been earned but not yet recorded. Some expenses have been incurred but no bill has arrived. Some cash received earlier hasn't been fully earned yet. Adjusting entries fix all of this — they bring account balances in line with economic reality before the statements go out. Every adjusting entry touches one balance sheet account and one income statement account. And here is a rule you can rely on: no adjusting entry ever involves cash. If your adjusting entry has cash in it, something is wrong.
learn.example:
At month-end, a company realizes it used $200 of supplies that were originally recorded as an asset.

Adjusting entry:
Debit  Supplies Expense  200
Credit Supplies          200

This moves the used portion from the balance sheet (Supplies asset) to the income statement (Supplies Expense), accurately reflecting what was consumed during the period.
keyPoints: Adjusting entries are always made at period-end, never during the period — they are a cleanup step before statements are prepared
Every adjusting entry affects one balance sheet account and one income statement account — never just one
Adjusting entries never involve cash — if your entry includes cash, it is not an adjusting entry

learn.explanation: When you pay for something before receiving the benefit — rent for next quarter, a year of insurance, a supply order you haven't used yet — you create a prepaid expense. It is an asset because the future benefit has not been consumed yet. Each period, as you actually use that benefit, you transfer a portion from the asset account to an expense account. A business owner who ignores prepaid expenses and just records the full cash payment as an expense immediately will see their expenses spike in the month of payment and be understated in every other month. Spreading it correctly gives you a much cleaner picture of real monthly costs.
learn.example:
October 1: A company prepays $6,000 for six months of rent.
Entry: Debit Prepaid Rent $6,000 / Credit Cash $6,000

October 31 adjusting entry (one month used = $1,000):
Debit  Rent Expense   1,000
Credit Prepaid Rent   1,000

Balance sheet after adjustment:
Prepaid Rent = $5,000 (five months remaining)
keyPoints: Cash paid in advance goes to an asset account first, then gets transferred to expense as you actually consume it
At period-end, always ask: how much of this prepaid has been used up during this period?
Without adjusting for prepaids, your expenses are understated and your assets are overstated — income looks better than reality

learn.explanation: Unearned revenue is the opposite problem from prepaid expenses — instead of paying in advance, your customer paid you in advance. Until you deliver the promised service or product, that money is not yours to claim as revenue. It sits on your balance sheet as a liability because you owe the customer something. As you deliver, you transfer from the liability to revenue. This shows up constantly in real business: annual software subscriptions, gym memberships, gift cards, magazine subscriptions, contractor deposits. A business that books all of these as revenue immediately is overstating income and misleading everyone who reads the statements.
learn.example:
A lawn care company collects $300 in advance for three months of service (June, July, August).

June 1: Debit Cash $300 / Credit Unearned Revenue $300

June 30 adjusting entry (one month's service performed = $100):
Debit  Unearned Revenue  100
Credit Service Revenue   100

Unearned Revenue balance decreases from $300 to $200.
keyPoints: Cash received before you have earned it is a liability — you owe your customer a product or service
Each period, recognize only the portion you have earned by delivering the promised service
Subscriptions, deposits, gift cards, and prepaid service contracts all create unearned revenue — very common in modern businesses

learn.explanation: Some expenses build up over time and don't come with a bill at exactly the right moment. Your employees worked the last three days of the month, but payroll doesn't run until next week. Interest on your loan accumulated all month, but the bank only charges you once a quarter. These costs are real — the obligation exists — but they have not been formally invoiced or paid yet. Accrued expenses are how you capture them. Without accrued expenses, your income statement understates costs and your balance sheet understates what you owe. Both misrepresentations make the business look better than it actually is.
learn.example:
Employees earn $4,000 in wages during the last few days of December, but payday is January 5.

December 31 adjusting entry:
Debit  Wages Expense    4,000
Credit Wages Payable    4,000

This records December's full labor cost on the income statement and shows the obligation to employees on the balance sheet.

January 5 when paid:
Debit  Wages Payable    4,000
Credit Cash             4,000
keyPoints: An accrued expense is owed and incurred but not yet paid — the obligation is real even without a cash payment or invoice
Wages payable, interest payable, and utility bills are the most common accrued expenses at month-end
Without accruals, your income statement understates expenses and your balance sheet understates liabilities — both misrepresentations inflate apparent profits

learn.explanation: Accrued revenue is the mirror image of accrued expenses. You have done the work and earned the money, but you have not sent the invoice yet or the customer has not paid. Lawyers finishing a case in late December, contractors completing a job the last week of the month, a bank earning interest on a loan — all of these generate real, earned revenue that should show up in the current period's income statement. If you skip the adjusting entry, you will understate revenue in the current period and overstate it in the next period when you finally invoice, which distorts trends and makes it harder to evaluate actual business performance.
learn.example:
A law firm completes $2,500 of legal work for a client on December 28 but will not send the invoice until January.

December 31 adjusting entry:
Debit  Accounts Receivable  2,500
Credit Legal Revenue         2,500

December's income statement now correctly includes this earned revenue, and the balance sheet shows the receivable owed by the client.
keyPoints: Accrued revenue is earned but not yet invoiced or collected — the income is real even without a cash receipt
Record a receivable and the revenue at period-end for any work already performed but not yet billed
Law firms, contractors, and consultants deal with this constantly — end-of-month work performed but not yet invoiced must be recorded

learn.explanation: When you buy a delivery truck for $40,000, you haven't spent $40,000 in the month of purchase — you've acquired an asset that will generate value over several years. Depreciation is how accounting spreads that cost across the years the truck is actually being used, matching the expense to the benefit. Each year you record depreciation expense on the income statement and reduce the book value of the asset. Crucially, depreciation is a non-cash expense — it reduces your reported income but no money leaves your account. This is why a company's cash flow can be significantly higher than its reported net income: depreciation reduces income without touching cash.
learn.example:
A company buys equipment for $10,000 with a 5-year useful life and no salvage value.
Straight-line depreciation: $10,000 ÷ 5 = $2,000/year

Annual adjusting entry:
Debit  Depreciation Expense        2,000
Credit Accumulated Depreciation    2,000

After year 2 balance sheet presentation:
Equipment                        $10,000
Less: Accumulated Depreciation   ($4,000)
Book Value                        $6,000
keyPoints: Depreciation is a non-cash expense — it reduces reported income without any cash leaving the business
Book value (cost minus accumulated depreciation) is an accounting number, not market value — they rarely match
Land is the only tangible asset that is never depreciated — everything else with a finite useful life gets depreciated

learn.explanation: After all adjusting entries have been recorded and posted, you run an adjusted trial balance — an updated list of all account balances that reflects every correction made for the period. This is the direct source for every number on your financial statements. Revenue and expense accounts from the adjusted trial balance go to the income statement. Asset, liability, and equity accounts go to the balance sheet. If the adjusted trial balance doesn't balance, you have an error in your adjusting entries and must find it before preparing any statements. The quality of your financial statements is only as good as the accuracy of this step.
learn.example:
Before adjusting entries:
Prepaid Insurance: $1,200 | Insurance Expense: $0

Adjusting entry (one month used):
Debit Insurance Expense $100 / Credit Prepaid Insurance $100

Adjusted trial balance:
Prepaid Insurance: $1,100 | Insurance Expense: $100

These updated balances are what appear on the financial statements.
keyPoints: The adjusted trial balance is prepared after all adjusting entries — it is the direct source for every number on the financial statements
Financial statements are built from the adjusted trial balance, not the unadjusted one — this step cannot be skipped
If the adjusted trial balance does not balance, find and fix the error before preparing any statements

learn.explanation: The income statement answers the most fundamental business question: did we make money this period? It shows all revenues earned, all expenses incurred, and the net difference — net income or net loss. For a service business it's straightforward: Revenue minus Expenses equals Net Income. For a business that sells products, there's a layer in between: Sales minus Cost of Goods Sold equals Gross Profit, and then you subtract operating expenses to get to net income. One critical thing to understand: net income is not the same as cash. You can be profitable on paper and still run out of cash to pay your bills. The income statement alone doesn't tell you that.
learn.example:
XYZ Consulting — Income Statement
For the Month Ended June 30

Service Revenue               $10,000
Less Expenses:
  Rent Expense      $1,000
  Wages Expense     $3,000
  Supplies Expense    $200
Total Expenses                 (4,200)

Net Income                    $ 5,800

The $5,800 flows to the statement of owner's equity.
keyPoints: The income statement tells you whether the business made money this period — not whether it has cash on hand
For merchandise companies: Revenue − COGS = Gross Profit, then Gross Profit − Operating Expenses = Net Income
Net income is not cash — a business can be profitable on paper and still go bankrupt if cash is not managed

learn.explanation: The statement of owner's equity is the bridge between the income statement and the balance sheet. It starts with the owner's equity balance at the beginning of the period, adds net income from the income statement, subtracts any amounts the owner withdrew, and produces the ending equity balance that goes onto the balance sheet. For a corporation, this becomes the statement of retained earnings, tracking how much of the cumulative profits have been kept in the business versus distributed to shareholders. The financial statements must be prepared in a specific order — income statement first, then equity, then the balance sheet — because each one feeds into the next.
learn.example:
XYZ Consulting — Statement of Owner's Equity
For the Month Ended June 30

Owner's Capital, June 1          $20,000
Add: Net Income                    5,800
Less: Owner Withdrawals           (2,000)

Owner's Capital, June 30         $23,800

This $23,800 appears in the equity section of the June 30 balance sheet.
keyPoints: This statement bridges the income statement and balance sheet — prepare it second, after the income statement and before the balance sheet
Owner withdrawals reduce equity just like a net loss does — taking money out of the business costs you in long-term equity
For a corporation, this becomes the statement of retained earnings — showing how profits were retained or distributed as dividends

learn.explanation: The balance sheet is a photograph of the business taken at one specific moment in time. It shows everything the business owns (assets), everything it owes (liabilities), and what's left for the owners (equity). Unlike the income statement which covers a period, the balance sheet is always dated to a single day. Assets are listed from most liquid to least liquid — cash first, then things that can be quickly converted to cash, then long-term assets like buildings and equipment. Liabilities are listed by maturity — bills due within a year come before long-term debt. The two sides must always balance: if they don't, something was recorded wrong.
learn.example:
XYZ Consulting — Balance Sheet
As of June 30

ASSETS
Cash                   $4,000
Accounts Receivable     2,000
Equipment              10,000
Total Assets          $16,000

LIABILITIES
Accounts Payable        1,200
Notes Payable           5,000
Total Liabilities       6,200

OWNER'S EQUITY
Owner's Capital        9,800
Total L + OE          $16,000 ✓
keyPoints: The balance sheet is a snapshot on one specific date — not a period summary like the income statement
Assets are listed from most to least liquid; liabilities are listed from shortest to longest maturity
The two sides must always equal — Assets = Liabilities + Owner's Equity, every time, without exception

learn.explanation: The statement of cash flows solves the mystery of why a profitable company can still run out of money. It tracks where cash actually came from and where it actually went during the period, organized into three buckets: operating activities (running day-to-day business), investing activities (buying and selling long-term assets), and financing activities (borrowing, repaying, and dealing with owners). Many companies have shown solid net income for years before suddenly going bankrupt — often because strong operating cash flow was masking deteriorating cash generation. Investors and lenders often consider operating cash flow a more reliable indicator of business health than net income.
learn.example:
For the month of June:

Operating Activities
Cash collected from customers      $9,000
Cash paid for expenses             (3,500)
Net cash from operating activities $5,500

Investing Activities
Purchased equipment                (2,000)

Financing Activities
Owner contribution                  1,000

Net increase in cash               $4,500
Beginning cash                          0
Ending cash                        $4,500 ✓ (matches balance sheet)
keyPoints: Profitable companies can still go bankrupt if they run out of cash — the cash flow statement shows the real picture
Three sections: operating (running the business), investing (buying and selling long-term assets), financing (debt and equity transactions)
Strong and growing operating cash flow that exceeds net income is a sign of high earnings quality

learn.explanation: At the end of every accounting period, all revenue and expense accounts need to be zeroed out so they start fresh in the new period. These are called closing entries. The net balance — all revenues minus all expenses — gets transferred to the permanent equity account (Retained Earnings or Owner's Capital), which carries forward forever. This is what makes your January income statement show only January's activity rather than everything since the company was founded. Assets, liabilities, and equity accounts are never closed — only temporary accounts (revenues, expenses, dividends) get zeroed out.
learn.example:
A company has: Service Revenue $10,000, Wages Expense $4,000, Rent Expense $1,000.

Entry 1 — Close revenue:
Debit Service Revenue $10,000 / Credit Income Summary $10,000

Entry 2 — Close expenses:
Debit Income Summary $5,000 / Credit Wages Expense $4,000, Credit Rent Expense $1,000

Entry 3 — Close Income Summary:
Debit Income Summary $5,000 / Credit Retained Earnings $5,000

All revenue and expense accounts are now $0.
keyPoints: Closing entries zero out all temporary accounts — revenues, expenses, and withdrawals — so each period starts fresh
Permanent accounts (assets, liabilities, equity) are never closed — they carry forward to the next period indefinitely
The net effect of closing moves net income into retained earnings, connecting the income statement to the balance sheet

learn.explanation: After closing entries are posted, you run one final trial balance to confirm everything is clean before the new period starts. This post-closing trial balance should contain only permanent accounts — assets, liabilities, and equity. If any revenue or expense account still has a balance, the closing entries were done incorrectly and must be redone. This step is also important because the balances here become the opening balances of the next period. Any error you leave uncorrected now will carry forward and corrupt every future period's records until someone finds and fixes it.
learn.example:
Post-Closing Trial Balance as of June 30:

Account                 Debit   Credit
Cash                    4,000
Accounts Receivable     2,000
Equipment              10,000
Accounts Payable                 1,200
Notes Payable                    5,000
Retained Earnings                9,800
Totals                 16,000   16,000 ✓

No revenue or expense accounts appear — they have been closed.
keyPoints: After closing, only balance sheet accounts (assets, liabilities, equity) should have non-zero balances
If any revenue or expense account still shows a balance, the closing entries were incomplete or incorrect
These balances become the opening balances for the next period — errors here carry forward until someone finds and corrects them

learn.explanation: Internal controls are the rules and procedures a business puts in place to protect its assets from theft, prevent fraud, and keep financial records accurate. The most powerful single control is separation of duties: never let one person handle a transaction from start to finish. If the same employee receives cash, records the receipt, and reconciles the bank statement, they have everything they need to steal and cover it up. The Association of Certified Fraud Examiners reports that businesses lose about 5% of revenue to fraud annually, and most fraudsters are trusted, long-term employees. Good internal controls are not a sign of distrust — they are a sign of professional management.
learn.example:
A small company implements separation of duties for cash receipts:
- Person A opens the mail and lists all incoming checks
- Person B records those checks in the accounting system
- Person C reconciles the bank statement monthly

Because three different people handle different parts of the process, no single employee could steal cash and cover it up without involving at least one other person.
keyPoints: Separation of duties is the single most effective internal control — no one person should handle a transaction from beginning to end
Internal controls are required by law for public companies under Sarbanes-Oxley, but every business benefits from implementing them
Controls reduce but cannot eliminate fraud — human judgment and collusion between employees can always defeat individual controls

learn.explanation: On almost any given day, your books will show a different cash balance than your bank statement — and both numbers can be correct. Timing differences are normal: you may have written checks that haven't cleared yet, or made deposits that haven't posted. A bank reconciliation works through these differences systematically to confirm you and the bank should both arrive at the same true balance. It also catches things that require journal entries: bank fees you didn't know about, an NSF check from a customer, interest earned. An independent person should always prepare the bank reconciliation — if the person handling cash also reconciles the bank statement, they have the ability to cover up a theft.
learn.example:
Company books show $5,200 cash. Bank statement shows $5,600.

Bank side: $5,600 − Outstanding checks $800 + Deposit in transit $400 = True balance $5,200
Book side: $5,200 − Bank service fee $20 + Interest earned $20 = True balance $5,200 ✓

Journal entries needed:
Debit Bank Fee Expense $20 / Credit Cash $20
Debit Cash $20 / Credit Interest Revenue $20
keyPoints: The bank's balance and your book balance will almost never agree on the same day — timing differences are normal and expected
Outstanding checks and deposits in transit explain most differences; bank fees and NSF checks require journal entries to fix
Someone independent of cash handling should prepare the bank reconciliation — this is a critical separation of duties control

learn.explanation: Every business needs small amounts of cash on hand for minor expenses — parking meters, office birthday cake, postage for one package. Managing these through a formal check process wastes everyone's time. A petty cash fund solves this: you set aside a small amount (say $200), assign one person to be the custodian, and require a receipt for every disbursement. When the cash runs low, you replenish it by writing one check to cover all the small expenses at once. The key rule: the Petty Cash account only changes when you first establish the fund or when you decide to change its size. Replenishment entries never touch the Petty Cash account — they record the individual expenses directly.
learn.example:
Establish a $200 petty cash fund:
Debit Petty Cash $200 / Credit Cash $200

Over the month, $80 is disbursed: $40 supplies, $25 postage, $15 miscellaneous.

Replenishment entry:
Debit Supplies Expense      40
Debit Postage Expense       25
Debit Miscellaneous Expense 15
Credit Cash                 80

Petty Cash account stays at $200.
keyPoints: The Petty Cash account only changes when you establish the fund or change its size — replenishment never touches it
Always require a receipt for every disbursement — cash plus receipts should always equal the total fund amount
Replenishment entries record all the individual expenses, not a single lump payment

learn.explanation: Cash is the asset most vulnerable to theft — it's liquid, anonymous, and hard to trace once it's gone. The most effective cash controls layer multiple safeguards: separate the person who handles cash from the person who records it, deposit all cash received daily rather than letting it accumulate, require pre-numbered checks so missing ones are immediately obvious, require two signatures on large checks, and have bank reconciliations performed by someone not involved in day-to-day cash handling. Most cash theft is small and incremental — a few hundred dollars a month from someone who knows the process well. Strong controls eliminate the opportunity before it becomes a habit.
learn.example:
A grocery store implements layered cash controls:
1. Cashier counts their register at start of shift
2. Shift supervisor performs a separate count
3. Store manager reconciles both counts against the POS system
4. All cash is deposited daily by the manager, never the cashier

With three independent counts and separation of deposit duties, a cashier would need to collude with both the supervisor and manager to steal undetected.
keyPoints: Deposit all cash received daily — cash sitting in a drawer overnight is an open invitation to theft
The person who handles cash should never be the same person who records it or reconciles the bank statement
Most cash fraud is committed by trusted, long-term employees — controls must apply equally to everyone regardless of tenure or seniority

learn.explanation: Most business-to-business transactions don't involve immediate cash payment. A supplier ships products and gives the buyer 30 days to pay. Under accrual accounting, the revenue is recorded the day the goods are delivered, not the day the check arrives. This creates an accounts receivable — a formal record of what customers owe you. Managing receivables well is one of the most important cash management tasks for any business. Customers who pay slowly tie up your working capital. The longer an account goes unpaid, the harder it becomes to collect. Many profitable businesses have been brought down by poor receivables management.
learn.example:
A company sells $3,000 of merchandise on credit, terms net/30.

Sale entry:
Debit  Accounts Receivable  3,000
Credit Sales Revenue         3,000

30 days later, customer pays in full:
Debit  Cash                  3,000
Credit Accounts Receivable   3,000
keyPoints: Record the revenue the day goods are delivered or services performed — not the day cash arrives
Accounts Receivable is an asset representing money customers owe you — it is real value, just not yet collected
The longer an account goes unpaid, the less likely you are to collect it — manage receivables actively and aggressively

learn.explanation: If you extend credit, some customers will not pay. The question is not whether you'll have bad debts, but how much. The allowance method requires you to estimate that amount at year-end and record it as bad debt expense — even before you know which specific customers will default. This matches the expense to the period when the credit sale was made, which is the fair way to measure that period's profitability. The Allowance for Doubtful Accounts is a contra-asset account that reduces the balance sheet value of A/R to what you actually expect to collect. When a specific customer is finally declared uncollectible and written off, no new expense is recorded — you already took that hit when you made the estimate.
learn.example:
Year-end: A/R balance is $100,000. Management estimates 3% will be uncollectible.

Adjusting entry:
Debit  Bad Debt Expense                3,000
Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts 3,000

Balance sheet presentation:
Accounts Receivable              $100,000
Less: Allowance for Doubtful Accts (3,000)
Net Realizable Value              $97,000

If a specific $500 account is later written off:
Debit Allowance $500 / Credit A/R $500 (no expense recorded)
keyPoints: Estimate bad debts at year-end even before you know which specific customers won't pay — matching the cost to the period of the sale
The Allowance for Doubtful Accounts reduces the balance sheet value of A/R to what you actually expect to collect
When you write off a specific account, no new expense is recorded — the expense was recognized at the time of the estimate

learn.explanation: The direct write-off method is the simpler alternative: you don't estimate anything in advance. You just wait until you know a customer isn't going to pay, then record the bad debt expense at that moment. The problem is timing — you might sell to a customer in December, include the revenue in December's income statement, and then find out in March that they have gone bankrupt. Under the direct write-off method, the bad debt expense lands in March, not December. This violates the matching principle and makes the income statement less reliable. GAAP prohibits this method for most companies, but the IRS actually requires it for tax purposes.
learn.example:
A customer who owed $500 has filed for bankruptcy and will never pay.

Write-off entry:
Debit  Bad Debt Expense       500
Credit Accounts Receivable    500

If the customer unexpectedly pays later (recovery):
Entry 1 — Reinstate the account:
Debit A/R $500 / Credit Bad Debt Expense $500

Entry 2 — Record cash receipt:
Debit Cash $500 / Credit A/R $500
keyPoints: The direct write-off method is simple but violates the matching principle — the expense lands in a different period than the revenue
GAAP prohibits the direct write-off method for most companies; the IRS requires it for income tax purposes
If a written-off account later pays, reverse the write-off first, then record the cash — two separate entries

learn.explanation: There are two main approaches to estimating how much bad debt expense to record. The percentage of sales method applies a single historical rate to total credit sales — quick and simple, but it focuses on the income statement and may let the allowance balance drift away from reality over time. The aging of accounts receivable method breaks your receivables into buckets by how long they have been outstanding and applies a higher uncollectibility rate to older accounts, because a 90-day-overdue invoice is dramatically harder to collect than a 15-day-overdue one. Most companies prefer the aging method because it produces a more accurate picture of what the A/R balance is actually worth.
learn.example:
Aging analysis:
0-30 days:   $60,000 × 1%  =   $600
31-60 days:  $20,000 × 5%  = $1,000
61-90 days:  $10,000 × 20% = $2,000
Over 90 days: $5,000 × 50% = $2,500
Total estimated uncollectible: $6,100

If Allowance already has a $1,000 credit balance:
Adjusting entry: Debit Bad Debt Expense $5,100 / Credit Allowance $5,100
(Bring allowance up to the required $6,100 balance)
keyPoints: The aging method is more accurate — older receivables carry higher uncollectibility rates because the longer they go unpaid, the harder they are to collect
The percentage of sales method focuses on matching expense to revenue; the aging method focuses on accurate balance sheet valuation
Always check the existing allowance balance before computing the adjusting entry — you need to bring it to the target balance, not record the full target as new expense

learn.explanation: A note receivable is a step up from an open account — it is a signed, written promise from the customer to pay a specific amount by a specific date, plus interest. Notes arise when a customer needs extended time to pay, when you're lending money to a related party, or when an overdue account is converted to a note to formalize the obligation. The note earns interest over its life, and you must recognize that interest income each period even if the cash hasn't arrived yet. This is another application of accrual accounting: the interest is earned over time, not just when the payment arrives.
learn.example:
July 1: A company accepts a $5,000, 6-month, 8% note.
Interest calculation: $5,000 × 8% × 6/12 = $200

December 31 maturity — entry:
Debit  Cash              5,200
Credit Notes Receivable  5,000
Credit Interest Revenue    200

If the year-end falls on September 30 (3 months in):
Adjusting entry:
Debit Interest Receivable $100 / Credit Interest Revenue $100
($5,000 × 8% × 3/12 = $100 earned but not yet received)
keyPoints: A note receivable is more secure than an open account — it is a signed legal document with specific payment terms
Interest accrues daily — record an adjusting entry at period-end for interest earned but not yet received
Formula: Principal × Annual Rate × (Time/12) for months, or Principal × Rate × (Days/360) for days

learn.explanation: The difference between perpetual and periodic inventory comes down to when you update the inventory records. Perpetual means you update after every single transaction — every purchase and every sale is tracked in real time. Walk into any modern retailer with barcode scanners and point-of-sale systems, and you're looking at perpetual inventory. Periodic means you only figure out what you have at the end of the period by doing a physical count. This was fine when everything was done by hand, but today virtually every business with meaningful inventory uses perpetual. The visibility alone is worth the system cost — you can't manage inventory you can't see.
learn.example:
A retailer sells a product that cost $30 for $50.

Perpetual system (two entries at sale):
Debit Cash $50 / Credit Sales Revenue $50
Debit Cost of Goods Sold $30 / Credit Inventory $30

Periodic system (one entry at sale):
Debit Cash $50 / Credit Sales Revenue $50
(COGS is calculated only at year-end after a physical count)
keyPoints: Perpetual systems update inventory records continuously — you always know exactly what you have on hand
Both systems still require a physical count at year-end to catch shrinkage from theft, damage, or counting errors
Almost all modern businesses use perpetual inventory — periodic is used only by very small or low-tech operations

learn.explanation: The full cost of getting inventory into your warehouse is what you record — not just the invoice price. Under the perpetual system, everything it takes to acquire and receive inventory goes into the Inventory account: the purchase price, freight charges to ship it to you, and any import duties or handling fees. This is called the cost principle. Later, when you sell the inventory, you transfer those same costs to Cost of Goods Sold. One important distinction: freight you pay to ship goods to your customers (freight-out) is a selling expense, not inventory cost. Mixing these up is a common mistake that distorts both your gross margin and your balance sheet.
learn.example:
A company purchases $10,000 of inventory on account with $300 freight.
Entry: Debit Inventory $10,300, Credit A/P $10,000, Credit Cash $300

$500 of defective goods are returned:
Debit A/P $500 / Credit Inventory $500

Net inventory cost recorded: $9,800
Final payment (no discount): Debit A/P $9,500 / Credit Cash $9,500
keyPoints: Under perpetual, all inventory acquisition costs — including freight-in — go directly into the Inventory account
Freight-in is an inventory cost; freight-out (to ship goods to your customers) is a selling expense — do not mix them up
Purchase returns reduce inventory cost immediately — record them when they happen to keep your records accurate

learn.explanation: Many suppliers offer a small discount if you pay early — terms like '2/10, net 30' mean you get 2% off if you pay within 10 days, otherwise you pay the full amount within 30 days. That 2% discount sounds small until you calculate the annualized cost of skipping it: roughly 36% per year. Almost no business has a better use for that cash than taking a 36% guaranteed return. From the seller's perspective, offering discounts is a way to get cash faster at the cost of slightly less revenue. Sales discounts are tracked as a contra-revenue account so you can see exactly how much your discount policy is costing you.
learn.example:
A company buys $10,000 of inventory on terms 2/10, net 30.

Purchase entry:
Debit Inventory $10,000 / Credit A/P $10,000

Payment within 10 days (discount taken — $200 savings):
Debit A/P $10,000
Credit Cash       9,800
Credit Inventory    200

Final net cost of inventory: $9,800
keyPoints: 2/10, net 30 means a 2% discount if paid within 10 days — the annualized cost of skipping this discount is approximately 36%
Always take purchase discounts if you have the cash — it is almost always the cheapest source of short-term return available
Sales discounts reduce net revenue — track them as a contra-revenue account to clearly see their impact on your pricing

topics.title: FOB Shipping Point vs. FOB Destination

learn.explanation: FOB terms answer one question: at exactly what moment does ownership of the goods transfer from seller to buyer? FOB Shipping Point means the transfer happens when goods leave the seller's dock — the moment they're handed to the carrier. From that point, the goods belong to the buyer, and the buyer pays the freight cost. FOB Destination means ownership doesn't transfer until the goods actually arrive at the buyer's location. This matters in practice because it determines who should include in-transit goods in their inventory count at year-end, who absorbs the loss if the goods are damaged in transit, and who pays the shipping bill.
learn.example:
Company A ships $5,000 of goods to Company B on December 30.
Terms: FOB Shipping Point.
Goods arrive at Company B on January 2.

Who includes these goods in December 31 inventory?
Company B — because ownership transferred on December 30 when goods left Company A's dock.

If terms were FOB Destination:
Company A would include them in December 31 inventory because they still own the goods in transit.
keyPoints: FOB Shipping Point: buyer owns the goods the moment they leave the seller's dock — buyer also pays the freight
FOB Destination: seller owns the goods until they arrive — seller pays the freight and absorbs any in-transit losses
For year-end inventory counts, FOB terms determine whether goods in transit belong to the buyer or the seller

learn.explanation: When prices change over time, the order in which you assign inventory costs to sales matters a lot to your income statement. FIFO — First-In, First-Out — assumes that the oldest inventory you purchased is the first to be sold. During a period of rising prices (which is most of the time), this means Cost of Goods Sold reflects the older, cheaper prices, making gross profit look higher. Ending inventory reflects the most recent, higher prices, making the balance sheet more realistic. FIFO is the most widely used method globally, is required under international accounting standards (IFRS), and produces the most accurate balance sheet.
learn.example:
Inventory layers:
Layer 1: 100 units purchased at $10 = $1,000
Layer 2: 100 units purchased at $12 = $1,200

Sell 150 units under FIFO:
COGS = (100 × $10) + (50 × $12) = $1,000 + $600 = $1,600
Ending Inventory = 50 units × $12 = $600

Goods available: $2,200 = COGS $1,600 + Ending Inventory $600 ✓
keyPoints: FIFO assumes the oldest inventory is sold first — during inflation this produces the lowest COGS and highest taxable income
Ending inventory under FIFO reflects the most recent prices — the most accurate balance sheet presentation
FIFO is required under IFRS and is the most common inventory method worldwide

learn.explanation: LIFO — Last-In, First-Out — assumes the most recently purchased inventory is sold first. During inflation, this pushes the newest, higher costs into COGS immediately, which raises your reported expenses and lowers taxable income. That tax saving is the entire reason many US companies use LIFO. The trade-off is a balance sheet that shows stale, unrealistically low inventory values because you are always carrying old, cheap costs. LIFO is a legitimate US GAAP option, but it is prohibited under IFRS, which matters for any company with international operations or investors. If a company switches from LIFO to FIFO, earnings can jump significantly.
learn.example:
Same inventory layers:
Layer 1: 100 units at $10 | Layer 2: 100 units at $12

Sell 150 units under LIFO:
COGS = (100 × $12) + (50 × $10) = $1,200 + $500 = $1,700
Ending Inventory = 50 units × $10 = $500

Compare to FIFO:
FIFO COGS = $1,600 (lower), FIFO Ending Inventory = $600 (higher)
LIFO produces $100 more COGS and $100 less ending inventory in this rising-cost example.
keyPoints: LIFO produces higher COGS and lower taxes during inflation — saving on taxes is the primary reason US companies choose it
LIFO ending inventory is chronically understated during inflation — balance sheet accuracy suffers significantly
LIFO is banned under IFRS — companies that report internationally cannot use it

learn.explanation: The weighted average method doesn't assume any particular order of sale — it just blends all the costs together into one average and applies that average to both what you sell and what remains. You calculate the average cost per unit by dividing total inventory cost by total units available, then use that single number consistently. The result is a COGS and an ending inventory value that fall between FIFO and LIFO, smoothing out price fluctuations. This method is common in industries where individual units are hard to distinguish — grain, oil, chemicals, and similar commodities.
learn.example:
Inventory available:
100 units × $10 = $1,000
100 units × $12 = $1,200
Total: 200 units, $2,200

Weighted Average Cost per unit = $2,200 ÷ 200 = $11.00

Sell 150 units:
COGS = 150 × $11 = $1,650
Ending Inventory = 50 × $11 = $550

Check: $1,650 + $550 = $2,200 ✓
keyPoints: Weighted average blends all costs into one per-unit average — it smooths out price fluctuations rather than tracking layers
Under the perpetual system, recalculate the weighted average after every new purchase
Results fall between FIFO (lower COGS) and LIFO (higher COGS) during rising prices

topics.title: Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value

learn.explanation: Inventory can lose value for many reasons: products go out of style, technology changes, goods get damaged, or markets collapse. The lower of cost or net realizable value (LCNRV) rule says you can never carry inventory on your balance sheet at more than what you could actually get for it. If you paid $50 for something you can now only sell for $33 after selling costs, you write it down to $33 now — not when you finally sell it. This is a core accounting principle: losses are recognized immediately when they become apparent, not deferred. Retailers dealing with seasonal merchandise, electronics companies, and fashion companies deal with this constantly.
learn.example:
A clothing retailer has $50,000 of seasonal inventory recorded at cost.
At year-end: estimated selling price $35,000, selling costs $2,000
Net Realizable Value (NRV) = $35,000 − $2,000 = $33,000

Since Cost ($50,000) > NRV ($33,000), write inventory down by $17,000:
Debit COGS (or Inventory Write-Down)  17,000
Credit Inventory                      17,000

Inventory now appears on the balance sheet at $33,000.
keyPoints: If inventory is worth less than you paid for it, write it down now — do not wait until you sell it
NRV equals estimated selling price minus the costs to complete and sell — not the same as current replacement cost
Write-downs increase cost of goods sold (or record a separate loss) and reduce inventory on the balance sheet

learn.explanation: Property, plant, and equipment — PP&E — are the physical assets a business uses to run its operations over multiple years: buildings, machinery, vehicles, computers, and land. You record PP&E at its full cost to acquire and get it ready for productive use, not just the sticker price. That means adding freight, installation charges, legal fees for titles, and site preparation to the purchase price. These costs are then spread over the asset's useful life through depreciation — except for land, which lasts forever and never gets depreciated. PP&E is often the single largest asset on a manufacturing or capital-intensive company's balance sheet.
learn.example:
A company purchases equipment for $50,000. Additional costs:
Shipping to location:    $2,000
Installation and testing: $3,000

Total cost to capitalize:
$50,000 + $2,000 + $3,000 = $55,000

Entry:
Debit Equipment $55,000 / Credit Cash $55,000

Depreciation will be calculated on the full $55,000 — all costs to get the asset ready for use are part of its recorded cost.
keyPoints: Record PP&E at the total cost to acquire and get it ready for use — not just the purchase price on the invoice
Land is never depreciated — it is the only tangible asset with an unlimited useful life
Book value (cost minus accumulated depreciation) is an accounting figure — it rarely reflects current market value

learn.explanation: When you spend money on a long-term asset, the key question is: does this cost extend the asset's useful life or add new capability, or does it merely maintain what you already have? Adding a new wing to a warehouse is a capital improvement — capitalize it and depreciate it. Fixing a broken window is a repair — expense it immediately. This distinction is critical because the choice directly affects your reported profit. Capitalizing costs you should expense makes your assets and profits look higher than they really are — in the short term. WorldCom's collapse was built on this exact fraud: they reclassified $3.8 billion of routine operating expenses as capital assets.
learn.example:
A company has two expenditures on a delivery truck:

1. Replace broken brake pads — $800 (restores to original condition)
   → Expense immediately:
   Debit Repair Expense $800 / Credit Cash $800

2. Install a refrigeration unit — $12,000 (adds new capability)
   → Capitalize and depreciate:
   Debit Equipment $12,000 / Credit Cash $12,000
keyPoints: Capitalize costs that extend useful life or add new capability; expense costs that merely maintain the asset's current condition
Capitalizing costs that should be expensed inflates assets, understates expenses, and overstates net income — this is a common vehicle for earnings manipulation
When in doubt, the more conservative choice is to expense — accountants apply conservatism to asset valuations

learn.explanation: Straight-line depreciation divides the depreciable cost of an asset evenly across every year of its useful life. The formula is: (Cost minus Salvage Value) divided by Useful Life. Salvage value is your estimate of what the asset will be worth when you're done using it. You stop depreciating when the book value reaches the salvage value — the asset is fully depreciated, even if it keeps running. Straight-line is the most commonly used method because it's simple, predictable, and appropriate for assets that provide roughly equal benefit each year — buildings, furniture, most equipment.
learn.example:
A delivery truck costs $40,000, has a 5-year useful life, and an estimated salvage value of $5,000.

Annual depreciation: ($40,000 − $5,000) ÷ 5 = $7,000/year

Annual adjusting entry:
Debit  Depreciation Expense         7,000
Credit Accumulated Depreciation     7,000

Balance sheet after year 3:
Truck (cost)                        $40,000
Less: Accumulated Depreciation     ($21,000)
Book Value                          $19,000
keyPoints: Annual depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life — equal expense every year
Salvage value is subtracted from cost before you divide — never depreciate below the salvage value
Straight-line is the most common method — use it for assets that deliver roughly equal benefit in each year of their life

learn.explanation: Some assets — computers, vehicles, technology equipment — lose most of their value and usefulness in the first few years of ownership. For these, an accelerated depreciation method makes more sense. Double-declining balance (DDB) applies a rate of 2 divided by the useful life to the asset's current book value each year. Because you're applying a constant rate to a shrinking base, the expense is highest in year one and decreases every year after. Two things to remember: salvage value is not subtracted from cost in the DDB calculation, but you must stop depreciating once book value hits salvage value.
learn.example:
Asset cost $10,000, 5-year life, $1,000 salvage value.
DDB rate = 2 ÷ 5 = 40%

Year 1: $10,000 × 40% = $4,000 depreciation | Book value = $6,000
Year 2: $6,000 × 40%  = $2,400 depreciation | Book value = $3,600
Year 3: $3,600 × 40%  = $1,440 depreciation | Book value = $2,160
Year 4: $2,160 × 40%  = $864  depreciation | Book value = $1,296
Year 5: Stop at $1,000 salvage → take only $296 depreciation
keyPoints: DDB applies a constant rate (2 ÷ useful life) to the declining book value each year — highest depreciation in year one
Do NOT subtract salvage value in the DDB calculation — but stop depreciating once book value reaches salvage value
Use accelerated methods for assets that lose value and usefulness quickly in their early years, like computers and vehicles

learn.explanation: For some assets, wear and tear is driven by usage, not the passage of time. A truck is not deteriorating because January turned into February — it's deteriorating because it drove 12,000 miles. For these assets, the units of production method ties depreciation directly to actual use: miles driven, units manufactured, hours operated. You calculate a depreciation rate per unit of activity and multiply by actual usage each period. This gives you low depreciation in slow years and high depreciation in busy years, which matches the economic reality of the asset's contribution much better than a time-based method.
learn.example:
A machine costs $50,000, has a $5,000 salvage value, and is expected to produce 90,000 units over its life.

Rate per unit: ($50,000 − $5,000) ÷ 90,000 = $0.50 per unit

Year 1: 20,000 units produced
Depreciation = 20,000 × $0.50 = $10,000

Year 2: 10,000 units produced (slower year)
Depreciation = 10,000 × $0.50 = $5,000

Depreciation directly reflects actual machine usage.
keyPoints: Depreciation expense varies directly with actual usage — low activity years produce low depreciation, busy years produce high depreciation
Calculate the rate per unit first: (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Total Estimated Units of Activity
Best for assets where wear is usage-driven, not time-driven — manufacturing equipment, vehicles tracked by mileage

learn.explanation: When you sell or retire a long-term asset, you need to do some cleanup in the books. First, update the depreciation right up to the date of sale — one final depreciation entry for the partial period. Then remove both the asset's original cost and all accumulated depreciation from the balance sheet. If you received more cash than the remaining book value, record a gain; if you received less, record a loss. These gains and losses appear on the income statement and affect your reported income for the period. Don't think of a gain on asset sale as operating success — it just means accounting had underestimated the residual value.
learn.example:
Equipment: cost $20,000, accumulated depreciation $15,000, book value $5,000.
Sold for $7,000 cash.

Gain = $7,000 proceeds − $5,000 book value = $2,000

Disposal entry:
Debit  Cash                         7,000
Debit  Accumulated Depreciation    15,000
Credit Equipment                   20,000
Credit Gain on Disposal             2,000
keyPoints: Update depreciation to the date of disposal before recording the sale — this step is mandatory and often missed
Gain or loss = Proceeds received minus book value at the date of disposal
Remove both the asset's original cost and all its accumulated depreciation from the books — both accounts must be cleared out

learn.explanation: Intangible assets are valuable things your company owns that you cannot physically touch — patents, trademarks, copyrights, franchise rights, customer relationships, and goodwill. If you bought an intangible asset from someone else, you record it at what you paid. If your own employees invented a patent or built a brand, those costs are generally expensed as incurred — you can't capitalize your own R&D under US GAAP. Intangibles with a definite life (a patent that expires in 10 years) are amortized straight-line over that life. Goodwill and intangibles with indefinite lives are never amortized — instead, you test them annually to see if they are still worth what is on the books.
learn.example:
A company purchases a patent for $60,000. The remaining legal life is 12 years but the asset is expected to be useful for only 10 years.

Use the shorter of legal life vs. useful life: 10 years.
Annual amortization: $60,000 ÷ 10 = $6,000

Annual entry:
Debit  Amortization Expense   6,000
Credit Patent                 6,000

After year 3, book value = $60,000 − (3 × $6,000) = $42,000
keyPoints: Intangibles with a definite useful life are amortized straight-line; indefinite-life intangibles like goodwill are tested for impairment instead
You can only capitalize purchased intangibles — internally developed intangibles are generally expensed as incurred
Goodwill only arises when you acquire another company and pay more than the fair value of its net identifiable assets

learn.explanation: Current liabilities are the bills coming due within the next year, and managing them is one of the most critical day-to-day responsibilities in any business. They include accounts payable (what you owe suppliers), wages payable (what you owe employees), interest payable (what you owe the bank), unearned revenue (services you've been paid for but not yet delivered), and the portion of any long-term loans due within 12 months. The current ratio — current assets divided by current liabilities — tells you whether you can cover your short-term obligations. A current ratio below 1.0 means you owe more in the next 12 months than you have available to pay — a serious warning sign.
learn.example:
Balance sheet current liabilities at December 31:
Accounts Payable           $8,000
Wages Payable               2,500
Unearned Revenue            1,200
Current Portion of Note Payable 5,000
Total Current Liabilities  $16,700

If Current Assets = $25,000:
Current Ratio = $25,000 ÷ $16,700 = 1.50
The company has $1.50 of current assets for every $1.00 owed short-term.
keyPoints: Current liabilities are due within one year — they represent the most immediate claims on your cash
The current ratio (Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities) tells you whether you can cover short-term obligations
A current ratio below 1.0 means current liabilities exceed current assets — a liquidity warning that demands immediate attention

learn.explanation: Accounts payable is money you owe to suppliers for goods and services you have already received but haven't yet paid for — it is the buyer's side of a credit transaction. Managing your payables strategically matters: paying too fast depletes cash unnecessarily; stretching too long damages supplier relationships and your credit terms. Accrued liabilities are a different category — they arise from adjusting entries at period-end to capture expenses you owe but haven't been billed for yet, like wages earned in the last few days of the month or interest that has accumulated on a loan. Both appear in current liabilities on the balance sheet.
learn.example:
A company receives a $3,000 utility bill on December 28 for December electricity usage but will not pay until January.

December 31 adjusting entry:
Debit  Utilities Expense    3,000
Credit Utilities Payable    3,000

January — payment made:
Debit  Utilities Payable    3,000
Credit Cash                 3,000
keyPoints: Accounts payable is what you owe suppliers for goods and services already received on credit
Accrued liabilities arise from adjusting entries — they capture expenses incurred but not yet formally billed or paid
Strategically managing when you pay payables affects your cash flow — paying too fast wastes cash, paying too slowly damages supplier relationships

learn.explanation: A note payable is a formal written promise to repay a borrowed amount plus interest — more legally binding than a simple account payable, and used for more significant obligations like bank loans and equipment financing. The interest on a note payable accrues continuously and must be recognized as an expense at period-end even if the bank doesn't charge you until the next quarter. This is important for accurate financial statements: the cost of borrowing belongs to the period in which you had the use of the money. If a portion of a long-term note matures within the next 12 months, that portion gets reclassified to current liabilities on the balance sheet.
learn.example:
November 1: Borrow $12,000 at 10% annual interest for 3 months.

Borrowing entry:
Debit Cash $12,000 / Credit Notes Payable $12,000

December 31 adjusting entry (2 months of interest):
Interest = $12,000 × 10% × 2/12 = $200
Debit Interest Expense $200 / Credit Interest Payable $200

February 1 — pay off note:
Debit Notes Payable   12,000
Debit Interest Payable   200
Debit Interest Expense   100  (Jan interest)
Credit Cash           12,300
keyPoints: A note payable is a formal legal document — more binding and more significant than an open account payable
Interest accrues daily — record an adjusting entry at period-end for any interest owed but not yet paid
Reclassify the current portion of long-term debt to current liabilities — this directly affects your current ratio and lender covenants

learn.explanation: Long-term liabilities are debts not due within the next year — typically mortgages, long-term bank loans, and bonds payable. Bonds allow a corporation to borrow millions of dollars from thousands of individual investors simultaneously, rather than from a single bank. The company promises to pay periodic interest (coupon payments) and return the principal at maturity. The price investors pay for the bond depends on whether the stated interest rate is above, below, or equal to the current market rate. Debt financing has an advantage over equity: interest expense is tax-deductible, effectively reducing the after-tax cost of borrowing.
learn.example:
A company issues $100,000 of 6% bonds at par value (stated rate = market rate).

Issuance:
Debit Cash $100,000 / Credit Bonds Payable $100,000

Annual interest payment:
Interest = $100,000 × 6% = $6,000
Debit Interest Expense $6,000 / Credit Cash $6,000

At maturity:
Debit Bonds Payable $100,000 / Credit Cash $100,000
keyPoints: Bonds let a company borrow from thousands of investors at once — useful for raising very large amounts of capital
Bond price depends on the relationship between the stated rate and the market rate — equal rates mean par, lower stated rate means discount
Interest expense on bonds is tax-deductible — debt has a built-in tax advantage over equity financing

learn.explanation: A contingent liability is a potential future obligation that depends on whether something uncertain happens — a pending lawsuit, a product recall, an environmental cleanup. You cannot record every possible bad outcome on the balance sheet or financial statements would be meaningless. The rule is straightforward: record it as a liability only if the loss is both probable (more likely than not) and can be reasonably estimated. If only one condition is met, disclose it in a footnote. Warranties are the most common application — every time you sell a product with a warranty, you already know some percentage will fail, and you book that estimated cost in the same period as the sale.
learn.example:
A company sells 1,000 products for $100 each. Based on experience, 5% will need warranty repairs averaging $20 each.

Estimated warranty liability: 1,000 × 5% × $20 = $1,000

Year-end accrual entry:
Debit  Warranty Expense               1,000
Credit Estimated Warranty Liability   1,000

When a customer actually brings in a product for repair:
Debit Estimated Warranty Liability / Credit Inventory or Cash
keyPoints: Record a contingent liability only when the loss is both probable AND can be reasonably estimated
If probable but not estimable, or only reasonably possible, disclose it in the footnotes — do not record it on the balance sheet
Warranty liabilities are the most common contingent liability — record them in the same period as the related sales to match costs properly

learn.explanation: A corporation raises equity capital by selling ownership stakes to investors in the form of stock. Common stockholders are the true owners — they vote on major decisions like electing the board and approving major transactions, and they receive whatever is left after everyone else is paid. Preferred stockholders give up voting rights in exchange for preferential treatment: they receive dividends before common stockholders and get paid out first in a liquidation. For the corporation, preferred stock is useful when you want to raise capital without diluting voting control. If preferred stock is cumulative, any dividends missed in prior years must be paid in full before common stockholders see a penny.
learn.example:
A corporation has 1,000 shares of $5 cumulative preferred stock and 10,000 shares of common stock. In a year with $20,000 available for dividends:

Preferred gets first priority:
1,000 shares × $5 = $5,000 to preferred stockholders

Remainder to common:
$20,000 − $5,000 = $15,000 ÷ 10,000 shares = $1.50 per common share
keyPoints: Common stockholders vote on major decisions; preferred stockholders generally do not — voting control is the key trade-off
Preferred stockholders receive dividends before common stockholders and have priority in liquidation
