Cumulative preferred means any missed dividends accumulate as dividends in arrears and must be fully paid before common stockholders receive anything

learn.explanation: Par value is a legal relic — a minimum value assigned to each share by the corporate charter, usually $0.01 or $1.00, that has almost nothing to do with the share's actual market value. When a company issues stock for more than par (which is virtually always), the par value goes into the Common Stock account and the excess goes into Additional Paid-In Capital (APIC). Together, these two accounts represent the total amount investors have contributed to the corporation in exchange for shares. Understanding this split matters because it helps you read the equity section of the balance sheet and understand exactly how much capital was raised and on what terms.
learn.example:
A corporation issues 1,000 shares of $1 par value common stock at $15 per share.
Total proceeds: 1,000 × $15 = $15,000

Issuance entry:
Debit  Cash                         15,000
Credit Common Stock (1,000 × $1)     1,000
Credit Additional Paid-In Capital   14,000

The $14,000 APIC is the amount received above par value.
keyPoints: Par value is an arbitrary legal number — it almost never reflects actual share value
The excess over par value goes to Additional Paid-In Capital (APIC) — most of the proceeds from stock issuance end up here
Common Stock (par) plus APIC together equal the total amount raised from investors through share issuance

learn.explanation: Treasury stock is shares the company has issued to the public and then bought back. Companies repurchase their own stock for several reasons: returning cash to shareholders (often more tax-efficient than dividends), boosting earnings per share by reducing the share count, providing shares for employee stock compensation plans, or defending against a hostile takeover. Treasury stock is always recorded at cost and shown as a negative number deducted from total stockholders' equity — it is not an asset. You cannot make a profit or loss on transactions involving your own stock; any difference between the repurchase price and the reissue price is adjusted through APIC.
learn.example:
A company repurchases 500 of its own shares at $20/share:
Debit Treasury Stock $10,000 / Credit Cash $10,000

Balance sheet equity section:
Common Stock            $ X,XXX
Additional Paid-In Capital  X,XXX
Retained Earnings          X,XXX
Less: Treasury Stock    (10,000)

Later, reissues 500 shares at $25/share:
Debit Cash $12,500 / Credit Treasury Stock $10,000 / Credit APIC $2,500
keyPoints: Treasury stock reduces total stockholders' equity — it is shown as a deduction from equity, not as an asset
When treasury stock is reissued at a price different from cost, adjust APIC — never record a gain or loss on your own stock
Stock buybacks are used to return cash to shareholders, increase EPS, or fund employee stock compensation programs

learn.explanation: Retained earnings is the running total of everything a corporation has ever earned, minus everything it has ever paid out to shareholders as dividends. It represents the portion of profits reinvested back into the business rather than distributed. A growing retained earnings balance means the company is building internal wealth. A declining balance — or a negative number called an accumulated deficit — signals the company has been paying out more than it earns, or operating at a loss. Dividends are not an expense; they never appear on the income statement. They are a distribution of profits, recorded as a deduction from retained earnings when declared and paid.
learn.example:
Beginning Retained Earnings:   $50,000
Add: Net Income                  30,000
Less: Dividends Declared        (10,000)
Ending Retained Earnings:       $70,000

On declaration date:
Debit Retained Earnings $10,000 / Credit Dividends Payable $10,000

On payment date:
Debit Dividends Payable $10,000 / Credit Cash $10,000
keyPoints: Retained earnings is the cumulative total of all net income ever earned minus all dividends ever paid — it grows every profitable year
Dividends are not an expense — they never appear on the income statement, only as a deduction from retained earnings
Three dividend dates: declaration (record the liability), record date (no entry required), payment date (pay the cash)

learn.explanation: A stock dividend and a stock split both increase the number of shares outstanding without distributing any cash. In a stock dividend, the company distributes additional shares proportionally to all shareholders — if you own 10% of the company, you still own 10% after the stock dividend, just through more shares. Total equity is unchanged; you're just slicing the pie into more pieces. A stock split does the same thing more dramatically (2-for-1, 3-for-1) and requires only a memo entry rather than a journal entry. These maneuvers are used to reduce share price to a more accessible trading range and signal management's confidence in the company's growth.
learn.example:
A company has 10,000 shares at $40 market price and declares a 10% stock dividend.

Shares issued: 10,000 × 10% = 1,000 new shares

Journal entry:
Debit Retained Earnings       40,000  (1,000 × $40 market)
Credit Common Stock            1,000  (1,000 × $1 par)
Credit Additional Paid-In Capital 39,000

Total stockholders' equity is unchanged — just reshuffled within equity.
keyPoints: Stock dividends and stock splits both increase shares outstanding but do not change total equity, total assets, or company value
Small stock dividend (under ~25%): debit Retained Earnings at market price; large stock dividend: debit at par value
A stock split requires only a memo entry — just note the new number of shares and reduced par value, no dollar amounts recorded

learn.explanation: The statement of cash flows exists because net income and cash are not the same thing. A company can post strong earnings while its actual cash position is deteriorating — because revenue was recognized on credit sales not yet collected, or because all earnings are being plowed back into equipment. The cash flow statement cuts through the accrual accounting adjustments and tells you exactly what happened to the cash. It is divided into three sections: operating (cash generated by running the business), investing (cash used to buy or sell long-term assets), and financing (cash flows with lenders and owners). The ending cash balance on this statement must tie exactly to the cash line on the balance sheet.
learn.example:
Summary statement of cash flows:

Operating activities         $ 20,000
Investing activities         (12,000)
Financing activities           3,000
Net increase in cash          11,000
Beginning cash                 5,000
Ending cash                  $16,000 ✓

The $16,000 must match the Cash line on the balance sheet.
keyPoints: The cash flow statement explains why net income and the change in cash are almost never the same number
Three sections: operating (running the business), investing (long-term assets), financing (debt and equity transactions)
Ending cash on the statement of cash flows must match the Cash balance on the balance sheet — this is a built-in verification check

learn.explanation: The indirect method is the way nearly every company in practice presents operating cash flows. It starts with net income from the income statement and works backward, adjusting for anything that affected net income but did not involve cash. Depreciation is the biggest adjustment: it reduced net income but required no cash outflow, so you add it back. Then you adjust for changes in working capital accounts. If accounts receivable grew, you earned more than you collected — subtract the difference. If accounts payable grew, you owed more than you paid — add it back. The result is actual cash generated by operations, which is often very different from reported net income.
learn.example:
Operating Activities (Indirect Method):
Net Income                        $30,000
Add: Depreciation (non-cash)        5,000
Subtract: Increase in A/R          (3,000)  ← earned but not collected
Add: Increase in Accounts Payable   2,000   ← owe but haven't paid
Net Cash from Operating Activities $34,000

The $4,000 difference from net income is explained by timing and non-cash items.
keyPoints: Start with net income, then adjust for non-cash items and working capital changes to arrive at actual operating cash
Depreciation is always added back — it reduced net income without any cash leaving the business
Increases in current assets use cash; increases in current liabilities provide cash — think about what actually happened to the money

learn.explanation: The direct method presents operating cash flows the way most people would intuitively describe them: cash received from customers, cash paid to suppliers, cash paid for wages, cash paid for taxes. It converts each income statement line item from accrual basis to cash basis by accounting for the related balance sheet changes. The FASB prefers it because it's more transparent — you can see exactly where the cash came from and where it went. But fewer than 2% of companies use it in practice because it requires significantly more work to prepare than the indirect method. Both methods produce identical totals for net cash from operating activities.
learn.example:
Operating Activities (Direct Method):
Cash received from customers   $95,000
  (Sales $100,000 − increase in A/R $5,000)
Cash paid to suppliers         (45,000)
  (COGS $50,000 − increase in A/P $5,000)
Cash paid for wages            (20,000)
Cash paid for rent              (8,000)

Net Cash from Operating Activities $22,000
keyPoints: The direct method shows actual cash received from customers and paid to suppliers, wages, rent, and taxes
Convert each income statement line to cash basis by adjusting for changes in related balance sheet accounts
Both methods produce the same net cash from operating activities total — only the presentation format differs

learn.explanation: The investing section of the cash flow statement shows how a company is allocating capital for the long term. Buying equipment, acquiring another company, or purchasing investments are cash outflows; selling assets or investments are cash inflows. Capital expenditures — the cash spent on property, plant, and equipment — are the single most important number here for most businesses. It tells you how much a company is investing in maintaining and growing its physical capacity. Free cash flow — operating cash flow minus capital expenditures — is one of the most widely used metrics in business valuation because it represents the cash available after the company has invested enough to sustain its operations.
learn.example:
Investing Activities:
Purchase of Equipment       ($50,000)
Purchase of Investments     ($20,000)
Proceeds from sale of truck   $8,000

Net Cash Used in Investing  ($62,000)

Free Cash Flow:
Operating cash flow $34,000 − CapEx $50,000 = ($16,000)

The company is investing more than its operations generate — typical for a growing company.
keyPoints: Buying long-term assets is a cash outflow; selling them is a cash inflow — both belong in investing activities
Capital expenditures (CapEx) is the most important line — it tells you how much the company is investing in its future capacity
Free Cash Flow = Operating Cash Flow minus CapEx — this is what investors use to value businesses and assess financial flexibility

learn.explanation: The financing activities section tells you how a company is funding itself and returning value to its stakeholders. Borrowing money and issuing stock bring cash in; repaying loans, paying dividends, and buying back stock send cash out. This section reveals a lot about a company's financial strategy and health. A mature, profitable company typically shows large cash outflows here — returning cash to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. A growing company often shows cash inflows — borrowing or issuing equity to fund expansion. A company that is consistently funding its core operations through financing rather than operations is living beyond its cash generation and should be watched carefully.
learn.example:
Financing Activities:
Proceeds from bank loan       $30,000
Repayment of long-term note  ($15,000)
Dividends paid to shareholders ($8,000)
Issuance of common stock      $25,000

Net Cash from Financing Activities $32,000

The company raised more cash from financing ($32,000) than it generated from operations — suggesting heavy reliance on external capital.
keyPoints: Borrowing money and issuing stock bring cash in; repaying debt, paying dividends, and buying back stock send cash out
Under US GAAP, dividends paid to shareholders are classified in financing activities, not operating activities
A company consistently funding operations through financing inflows rather than operating cash flow is raising a serious red flag

learn.explanation: Reading a single financial statement in isolation tells you very little. The real insight comes from comparison — either across time (horizontal analysis) or across line items as a percentage of a base figure (vertical analysis). Horizontal analysis shows you the trajectory: are revenues growing? Are expenses growing faster or slower than revenues? Is net income growing as a percentage of revenue, or shrinking? Vertical analysis converts every line to a percentage (usually of sales for the income statement, or total assets for the balance sheet), so you can compare your cost structure to a competitor of any size, or track structural shifts in your own business over time.
learn.example:
Horizontal Analysis:
Sales Year 1: $100,000 | Year 2: $120,000 | Change: +$20,000 (+20%)
Net Income Year 1: $10,000 | Year 2: $14,000 | Change: +$4,000 (+40%)
Net income grew twice as fast as sales — a very positive sign.

Vertical Analysis (Year 2):
Sales: $120,000 = 100%
COGS: $72,000 = 60%
Gross Profit: $48,000 = 40%
Net Income: $14,000 = 11.7%
keyPoints: Horizontal analysis tracks trends over time — look for revenues and net income growing faster than expenses
Vertical (common-size) analysis removes size differences so you can compare yourself to competitors regardless of scale
Look for consistent multi-year patterns, not single-year spikes — one-time events can distort any individual year's numbers

learn.explanation: Liquidity ratios answer the most urgent question about any business: can you pay your bills? The current ratio looks at all current assets divided by all current liabilities — a broad view of short-term coverage. The quick ratio is tougher: it strips out inventory and prepaid expenses, which may not convert to cash quickly, and divides only truly liquid assets by current liabilities. A manufacturer sitting on months of inventory might have a healthy current ratio but a scary quick ratio if a customer cancels a big order. Lenders typically require borrowers to maintain minimum current ratios as part of their loan covenants — dropping below the minimum can trigger early repayment.
learn.example:
Current Assets: Cash $10,000, A/R $15,000, Inventory $20,000, Prepaid $2,000 = $47,000
Current Liabilities: $25,000

Current Ratio: $47,000 ÷ $25,000 = 1.88
Quick Ratio: ($10,000 + $15,000) ÷ $25,000 = 1.00

The current ratio looks strong, but the quick ratio of exactly 1.0 reveals that the company's short-term liquidity depends heavily on converting inventory to cash.
keyPoints: Current ratio above 1.0 is the minimum — most healthy businesses aim for 1.5 to 2.0 to maintain a comfortable safety margin
The quick ratio excludes inventory and prepaids — if it is much lower than the current ratio, the company is highly dependent on inventory sales
Working capital (Current Assets minus Current Liabilities) is the dollar cushion available for day-to-day operations

learn.explanation: Profitability ratios tell you how efficiently a business converts revenue and assets into profit. Gross margin shows you production efficiency and pricing power — if it's falling, either your costs are rising faster than you can raise prices, or you're discounting too aggressively. Net margin is the bottom line — how much of every dollar of sales survives to become profit after all costs and taxes. Return on assets answers a different question: given everything the company owns, how much profit does it generate? Return on equity answers the most important question for shareholders: given the capital shareholders have invested, what return are they earning?
learn.example:
Net Sales: $500,000 | COGS: $300,000 | Gross Profit: $200,000
Operating Expenses: $100,000 | Net Income: $75,000
Total Assets: $400,000 | Equity: $250,000

Gross Margin:   $200,000 ÷ $500,000 = 40%
Net Margin:      $75,000 ÷ $500,000 = 15%
Return on Assets: $75,000 ÷ $400,000 = 18.75%
Return on Equity: $75,000 ÷ $250,000 = 30%
keyPoints: Gross margin reveals production efficiency and pricing power — a declining gross margin is an early warning sign of trouble
Net margin is the bottom line; ROA and ROE show whether you are using assets and owner capital efficiently
Always compare ratios to industry benchmarks and your own prior years — a ratio in isolation tells you almost nothing

learn.explanation: Solvency ratios tell you whether a company can survive over the long run by meeting its debt obligations. The debt-to-equity ratio shows how much of the business is funded by creditors versus owners — a high ratio means the company is highly leveraged, which amplifies both gains and losses. The times interest earned ratio is the most direct measure of debt safety: how many times can operating income cover the annual interest bill? Below 2.0 is uncomfortable; below 1.0 means the company can't even cover its interest costs from operations. Bond rating agencies, banks, and sophisticated investors scrutinize these ratios before committing any capital.
learn.example:
Total Liabilities: $300,000 | Total Equity: $200,000
Total Assets: $500,000 | Operating Income: $80,000 | Interest Expense: $20,000

Debt-to-Equity: $300,000 ÷ $200,000 = 1.5
  (Creditors provided $1.50 for every $1.00 of equity)
Times Interest Earned: $80,000 ÷ $20,000 = 4.0 times
  (Comfortable — covers interest 4 times over)
Debt Ratio: $300,000 ÷ $500,000 = 60%
keyPoints: High debt-to-equity means high financial risk — leverage amplifies both gains and losses, and a downturn can quickly become a crisis
Times interest earned below 2.0 is a danger zone — it means the company has little cushion between operating income and its mandatory interest payments
Creditors and rating agencies focus heavily on solvency ratios — low solvency scores mean higher interest rates on any future borrowing

learn.explanation: Efficiency ratios measure how well a company squeezes revenue out of its assets. Inventory turnover tells you how fast you are selling your product — if competitors turn inventory 12 times a year and you turn it 4 times, you are tying up three times as much working capital for the same level of sales. Days sales outstanding measures how quickly customers pay — if it is creeping up quarter over quarter, your collections process may be breaking down or you are extending credit to riskier customers. Asset turnover measures overall productivity of the asset base. Improving efficiency ratios is one of the most powerful levers management has to generate more return without deploying more capital.
learn.example:
Net Sales: $600,000 | COGS: $360,000
Average Inventory: $60,000 | Average A/R: $50,000
Average Total Assets: $400,000

Asset Turnover: $600,000 ÷ $400,000 = 1.5 times
Inventory Turnover: $360,000 ÷ $60,000 = 6.0 times
Days in Inventory: 365 ÷ 6.0 = 61 days
Receivables Turnover: $600,000 ÷ $50,000 = 12.0 times
Days Sales Outstanding: 365 ÷ 12 = 30 days to collect
keyPoints: Inventory turnover measures how fast you sell — low turnover means capital is tied up in sitting inventory, which is expensive
Rising days sales outstanding is an early warning of collection problems or deteriorating credit quality among your customers
Asset turnover shows how much revenue each dollar of assets generates — improving this without adding assets directly improves ROA
